topic: Unit 2: Listening, Reading and Use of French
subject: french

items.q: When does the past participle agree with avoir verbs?
items.correct: When the direct object comes BEFORE the verb (la pomme que j'ai mangée).
items.why: Students learn 'avoir = no agreement' as a blanket rule. The exception is PRECEDING DIRECT OBJECT: 'Je l'ai mangée' (la pomme) requires the participle to agree with the object pronoun.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.
A classic faux ami. Actuellement looks like 'actually' but means CURRENTLY. To say 'actually' use en fait or en réalité. These false friends regularly cost marks.

items.q: Who directed À bout de souffle (1960)?
items.why: It's Godard. “Truffaut” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: passé simple. “passé composé” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Les Quatre Cents Coups released?
items.why: Exact answer: 1959. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
